class.name: PlatformVideoStream
class.brief_description: [VideoStream] resource for hardware-decoded, zero-copy native video playback.
class.description: A [VideoStream] that plays a native video clip through the platform's hardware decoder (AVFoundation on macOS, Windows Media Foundation on Windows) and presents frames to the GPU with no CPU copy. The decoded NV12 surface is imported via [code]RenderingDevice.texture_create_from_extension[/code] and converted to RGB in a single compute pass into an engine-owned [Texture2DRD] returned from the playback's texture; Godot never samples the decoder surface directly. Drop a recognised clip ([code].mp4[/code], [code].mov[/code], [code].m4v[/code]) onto a stock [VideoStreamPlayer] to play it. Requires Godot 4.4+ and a [b]Forward+[/b] or [b]Mobile[/b] renderer (RenderingDevice); the Compatibility/OpenGL renderer is not supported. The v1 decode scope covers H.264 and HEVC (Main/Main10) video, MP4/MOV containers, NV12 / x420 / BT.709 / BT.2020 / PQ / HLG, and AAC stereo audio. 10-bit sources negotiate a matching 10-bit decoder output — [code]x420[/code] biplanar on macOS, [code]P010[/code] on Windows — for full precision; the conversion shader normalises 10-bit payloads in 16-bit GPU words. On Windows, the default CPU-Copy Import Path (stock Vulkan driver) reaches the GPU via a readback rather than the zero-copy import macOS uses. PQ/HLG clips tone-map to watchable SDR by default, or output scene-linear HDR when the stream's output mode is set to HDR. VP9/AV1 remain out of scope; multi-track AAC audio is supported — enumerate tracks with [method get_audio_tracks] and select one via [code]VideoStreamPlayer.audio_track[/code], either before [code]play()[/code] or mid-playback. Mixed-sample-rate clips are a documented limitation: the first audio track's sample rate wins, and a mid-stream switch to a track with a differing rate is refused.

method.name: get_audio_tracks
method.description: Returns an [Array] of [Dictionary] objects describing the clip's audio tracks. Each dictionary contains the following string keys: [code]language[/code] — BCP 47 language tag (may be empty), [code]name[/code] — human-readable display name (may be empty), [code]channels[/code] — number of audio channels (int), [code]sample_rate[/code] — sample rate in Hz (int), [code]default[/code] — whether this is the container's default track (bool). Array position corresponds to the track index accepted by [code]VideoStreamPlayer.audio_track[/code]. The probe is lazy: the clip is opened on the first call and the result is cached for subsequent queries. Set [code]VideoStreamPlayer.audio_track[/code] to the desired index before calling [code]play()[/code] to pre-select an audio track. An out-of-range index reports an error and falls back to the default (track 0).

method.name: hdr_decode_supported
method.description: Returns [code]true[/code] when the current platform supports hardware decoding of 10-bit (and HDR) video, matching the source's bit depth instead of down-converting to 8-bit. On macOS, [method hdr_decode_supported] always returns [code]true[/code] because the Metal-accelerated VideoToolbox can produce 10-bit biplanar surfaces (x420) that the Metal surface importer imports directly (zero-copy). On Windows, it also returns [code]true[/code]: Media Foundation negotiates 10-bit (P010) decoder output for 10-bit HEVC sources, and all three Windows Import Paths handle P010 — the default CPU-Copy Import Path (stock Vulkan driver) splits it into R16/RG16 planes and reaches the GPU via a readback, while the zero-copy DXGI/Vulkan and D3D12 Import Paths import the same P010 surface directly as 16-bit (R16/RG16) plane views with no CPU readback. On Linux, this returns [code]false[/code] — only 8-bit NV12 decode is supported there in this release. Call this before loading a clip to decide whether to offer HDR playback options in the UI, or to present an informative message when the clip's bit depth exceeds the platform's decode capability.

members.member: Selects the pipeline's output mode: [code]0[/code] for SDR (RGBA8) or [code]1[/code] for HDR (RGBA16F, scene-linear, 1.0 = 203-nit Reference White). Mirrors [member PlatformVideoStreamPlayback.output_mode] on every playback instantiated from this stream — set it here rather than on the playback directly so the stream resource and its live playbacks stay in sync.
